title: The Supplement Quality Crisis: What Your Label Isn't Telling You
excerpt: Nootropics Depot published a white paper exposing how supplements can pass quality tests while containing almost no active compounds. Here's the backstory and what to look for.
bottomLine: The supplement industry has a real quality problem, and it's not going away on its own. Until regulations catch up, the best defense is knowing what to look for: standardized bioactive content (not just extract ratios), transparent lab testing, and companies willing to publish their methodology. If a product only tells you the species and the ratio, that's not enough.

paragraphs: Here's something that should bother anyone who takes supplements seriously: a product can pass every standard quality test the industry requires and still contain almost none of the compounds that actually do anything.
That's the core claim in a technical white paper published by Nootropics Depot in early 2026, and the evidence they present is hard to argue with.

sections.heading: How We Got Here
paragraphs: The supplement industry in the United States operates under the Dietary Supplement Health and Education Act (DSHEA), passed in 1994. Under DSHEA, supplements don't need FDA approval before going to market. Manufacturers are responsible for ensuring their products are safe and properly labeled, but the bar for "properly labeled" is lower than most people realize.

For botanical supplements — things like ashwagandha, tongkat ali, or bacopa — the standard quality checks involve confirming the right plant species was used. This is typically done through DNA barcoding or chromatographic fingerprinting. Both methods answer the question: "Is this the right plant?" What they don't answer is: "Does this product contain enough of the active compounds to actually work?"

That gap has been known in the industry for years. But it became a bigger problem as demand for botanical nootropics surged. When tongkat ali went from a niche Southeast Asian herb to a mainstream biohacking supplement (thanks largely to Andrew Huberman's podcast coverage starting in 2022), supply chains stretched to meet demand. And quality suffered.

sections.heading: The Extract Ratio Problem
paragraphs: Walk through any supplement aisle and you'll see extract ratios: 10:1, 50:1, 100:1, 200:1. The implication is clear — a 200:1 extract should be 200 times more concentrated than raw plant material. More concentrated equals more potent, right?

Not necessarily. Nootropics Depot's white paper tested multiple tongkat ali products claiming 100:1 and 200:1 extract ratios. Many of them contained eurycomanone (the primary bioactive compound in tongkat ali) at levels equal to or below what you'd find in unprocessed root material. In other words, the "200:1 extract" had the same amount of active compound as the raw plant.

How is this possible? The answer is something called "spent marc." After a manufacturer extracts the valuable compounds from plant material, the leftover residue is called marc. Some suppliers take this spent marc — which still passes species identification tests — and sell it as extract. It's the right plant. It's technically an extract. But the useful compounds are already gone.

sections.heading: This Isn't Just Tongkat Ali
paragraphs: The white paper focuses on tongkat ali as a case study, but the same problem applies across botanical supplements. Ashwagandha products vary wildly in withanolide content. Ginseng products show huge differences in ginsenoside levels. Curcumin supplements range from therapeutic doses to negligible amounts, depending on the source.

The reason is structural: the tests that are standard in the industry confirm identity, not potency. As long as the DNA matches and the chromatographic fingerprint looks right, the product passes. Nobody is required to measure the specific compounds that drive the effects people are buying the supplement for.

sections.heading: What Good Testing Looks Like
paragraphs: Nootropics Depot is advocating for what they call "bioactive standardization" — testing that measures the specific active compounds using validated HPLC (High-Performance Liquid Chromatography) or UPLC methods. Instead of saying "this is tongkat ali, 200:1 extract," a properly tested product would say "this contains X mg of eurycomanone per serving, verified by HPLC."

Some companies already do this. When you see a product labeled "standardized to 5% withanolides" (for ashwagandha) or "standardized to 95% curcuminoids" (for curcumin), that's bioactive standardization at work. The number tells you exactly how much of the active compound you're getting.

The practical takeaway for consumers: ignore extract ratios. They're essentially meaningless without corresponding bioactive data. Instead, look for products that specify the active compound and its concentration. If a company can't or won't tell you the withanolide, eurycomanone, or ginsenoside content of their product, consider that a red flag.

sections.heading: Where the Industry Goes From Here
paragraphs: Mandatory bioactive standardization would solve most of these problems overnight. But regulation moves slowly, especially in the supplement space where industry lobbying has historically resisted tighter controls.

In the meantime, the market is self-correcting somewhat. Companies that invest in transparent testing — publishing certificates of analysis, using third-party labs, standardizing to specific bioactives — are winning consumer trust. The biohacking community, to its credit, has been pushing hard for this kind of transparency for years.

If you're building a nootropic stack, quality testing is as important as choosing the right compounds. A well-designed stack with poorly sourced supplements won't deliver the results you're optimizing for.
